analysis.question: When was the stale-price bug introduced, and why?
analysis.headline: The March 27 price cache introduced the stale-price failure
analysis.finding: The bug was most likely introduced by event-8c19a37 on 2026-03-27. To address OC-44 latency, commit 8c19a37 cached product prices without an invalidation path. Event-b24472a later confirmed checkout could retain an old price after catalog updates.

causal_chain.explanation: Commit 4d1810a recorded repeated pricing latency in large carts, creating pressure to reduce product lookups.
Commit 8c19a37 added an in-memory price cache with no invalidation path; this is the most likely introduction of the stale-price bug.
Commit b24472a recorded OC-52: checkout retained an old price after a catalog update.
Commit 3b1b4d7 disabled cache reads to restore correct totals while a safe design was prepared.
Commit 1509f33 reintroduced caching with catalog-version keys and explicit invalidation.
Commit a095037 added a regression test proving that a new catalog version cannot reuse an old cached price.

missing_evidence: Production telemetry showing the exact first stale-price occurrence is absent.
The evidence does not quantify affected orders, customer impact, or overcharge and undercharge amounts.
Production-load results for the versioned cache are absent.
risks: Catalog callers must increment the catalog version whenever a product price changes.
Cache performance under production load still requires monitoring.
